# Creating and Running Node Apps

# 1\. Intro to Node.js

Node.js is an asynchronous event driven JavaScript runtime environment.  
It’s NOT a programming language.  
It’s just the runtime environment that runs JavaScript programs.   
It allows running JavaScript on a terminal.

In this tutorial, we’ll install Node packages and run Node apps from your local computer.

We’ll learn:

1) Creating and running node apps in your local computer  
2) Installing Node packages using npm  
3) Creating and checking the package.json file  
4) Running a node app from the terminal window

# 2\. Creating and Running Node apps

1. Install/update Node. Go to: [https://nodejs.org/en/download/](https://nodejs.org/en/download/)  
   Download and install the latest version of your operating system.   
   It will also install npm.

2. Each Node app should be included within its own folder. Create a folder called “**firstNodeApp**” somewhere on your computer (e.g., within the “Desktop” or “Documents” folder). Open a Terminal window and navigate to the “firstNodeApp” folder  (using the “cd” command).

3. Run the following command to initialize the node app:  
   **`npm init`**

   ![][image1]

   It will run an interactive utility that will ask you for the basic information to be included in the package.json file. Keep in mind that the app name should be in lowercase.  To use the default values, just hit enter.

   All node apps must have a package.json file. This file includes metadata about the app, such as author, version, description, license, and more importantly all different dependencies (all other packages) that it uses.

   Alternatively, you can accept all default values  by initializing the app using:  
   **`npm init -y`**

4. Within the “**firstNodeApp**” folder, create a file called “**index.mjs”.** It can be any other name.

5. On the “**index.mjs**” file, just output something to the console, such as:

   `let vowels = ["a", "e", "i", "o", "u"];`  
   `console.log(vowels);`  
   

6. Run the program by typing the following command in the terminal window:

   **`node index.mjs`**  
     
   You should be able to see the output in the console.

   The Node programs can include most of the JavaScript code you’re already familiar with such as variable declaration, control structures, arrays, objects, and functions. However, given that Node doesn’t have access to a browser by default, the code must not include any reference to the “document” such as document.querySelector or use alert()  
     
7. Add the following function and run the code. You should see the corresponding output in the console.  
   ![][image2]

8. Installing a Node Package  
   There are hundreds of packages (or libraries) that you can install and run in Node.  To install modules, you can use the  “npm” command. “npm” stands for Node Package Manager.   
     
   **npm** is the world’s largest software registry. You can search for npm packages at: [https://www.npmjs.com/](https://www.npmjs.com/)

   Let’s install the “[underscore](https://underscorejs.org/)” package to be able to shuffle the array.

   From the **terminal window**, type:  
   npm install underscore  
     
   Alternatively, you can use the “i” shortcut  
   After hitting enter, you should see that the package is being installed.  
     
     
     
   When installing the first node package, you’ll notice that a **package.json** file is added to the app folder. This file keeps track of all the dependencies (packages needed by the app). Open it so you can see that a dependency was added.

9. Import the underscore package into the **index.mjs** file.  
   Update your app using the code below to import the underscore package.  
   When running the program, you should see a random list of vowels.

   `import _ from 'underscore';`  
     
   `let vowels = ["a", "e", "i", "o", "u"];`  
   `vowels = _.shuffle(vowels);`  
     
   `displayVowels();`  
     
   `function displayVowels() {`  
   	`for (let vowel of vowels){`  
   		`console.log(vowel);`  
   	`}`  
   `}`  
     
   ![][image3]

   # Review:

   When installing a new node app from a terminal window:

1. Create a new folder  
2. Run “npm init \-y” to create the package.json file  
3. Install as many npm packages as needed by running:  
    “npm install *package\_name*”  
           Note: You could use just "i" instead of "install"  
4. Create an “**index.mjs**” file   
5. Import the packages and refer to the package documentation to use it properly  
6. Run the app by typing   
       **node index.mjs**
